source: PubMed via NCBI E-utilities (claude.ai PubMed MCP)

queries.query_string: ("multiparametric MRI" OR "mpMRI" OR "PI-RADS") AND "prostate cancer" AND ("targeted biopsy" OR "diagnostic accuracy") AND ("sensitivity" AND "specificity")
"PI-RADS" prostate AND ("clinically significant" OR "Gleason") AND "sensitivity" AND "specificity" AND ("PI-RADS 3" OR "PI-RADS >=3")

included_records.title: Diagnostic accuracy of multi-parametric MRI and TRUS biopsy in prostate cancer (PROMIS): a paired validating confirmatory study.
included_records.abstract: Men with high serum prostate specific antigen usually undergo transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy (TRUS-biopsy). TRUS-biopsy can cause side-effects including bleeding, pain, and infection. Multi-parametric magnetic resonance imaging (MP-MRI) used as a triage test might allow men to avoid unnecessary TRUS-biopsy and improve diagnostic accuracy. We did this multicentre, paired-cohort, confirmatory study to test diagnostic accuracy of MP-MRI and TRUS-biopsy against a reference test (template prostate mapping biopsy [TPM-biopsy]). Men with prostate-specific antigen concentrations up to 15 ng/mL, with no previous biopsy, underwent 1.5 Tesla MP-MRI followed by both TRUS-biopsy and TPM-biopsy. Clinically significant cancer was defined as Gleason score >=4+3 or a maximum cancer core length 6 mm or longer. We enrolled 740 men, 576 of whom underwent 1.5 Tesla MP-MRI followed by both TRUS-biopsy and TPM-biopsy. On TPM-biopsy, 408 (71%) of 576 men had cancer with 230 (40%) of 576 patients clinically significant. For clinically significant cancer, MP-MRI was more sensitive (93%, 95% CI 88-96%) than TRUS-biopsy (48%, 42-55%; p<0.0001) and less specific (41%, 36-46% for MP-MRI vs 96%, 94-98% for TRUS-biopsy; p<0.0001).

included_records.title: Use of prostate systematic and targeted biopsy on the basis of multiparametric MRI in biopsy-naive patients (MRI-FIRST): a prospective, multicentre, paired diagnostic study.
included_records.abstract: In this prospective, multicentre, paired diagnostic study, done at 16 centres in France, we enrolled patients aged 18-75 years with prostate-specific antigen concentrations of 20 ng/mL or less, and with stage T2c or lower prostate cancer. Eligible patients had been referred for prostate multiparametric MRI before a first set of prostate biopsies. The primary outcome was the detection of clinically significant prostate cancer of International Society of Urological Pathology grade group 2 or higher (csPCa-A). Between July 15, 2015, and Aug 11, 2016, we enrolled 275 patients. 24 (9%) were excluded from the analysis. 53 (21%) of 251 analysed patients had negative (Likert <=2) multiparametric MRI. csPCa-A was detected in 94 (37%) of 251 patients. 13 (14%) of these 94 patients were diagnosed by systematic biopsy only [MRI-/csPCa+], 19 (20%) by targeted biopsy only, and 62 (66%) by both techniques. csPCa-A would have been missed in 5.2% (95% CI 2.8-8.7) of patients had systematic biopsy not been done.

included_records.title: The Additive Diagnostic Value of Prostate-specific Membrane Antigen Positron Emission Tomography Computed Tomography to Multiparametric Magnetic Resonance Imaging Triage in the Diagnosis of Prostate Cancer (PRIMARY): A Prospective Multicentre Study.
included_records.abstract: Multiparametric magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is validated for the detection of clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa). A prospective multicentre phase II imaging trial was conducted. A total of 296 men were enrolled with suspected prostate cancer, with no prior biopsy or MRI. In all, 291 men underwent MRI, pelvic-only PSMA, and systematic +/- targeted biopsy. csPCa was any International Society of Urological Pathology (ISUP) grade group >=2 malignancy. Of the patients, 56% (n=162) had csPCa; 67% had PI-RADS 3-5, 73% were PSMA positive, and 81% were combined PSMA+MRI positive. Combined PSMA+MRI improved NPV compared with MRI alone (91% vs 72%, p<0.001). Sensitivity also improved (97% vs 83%, p<0.001); however, specificity was reduced (40% vs 53%, p=0.011) [for combined vs MRI alone]. MRI-alone arm: Sens 83%, Spec 53%.

included_records.title: Optimized MRI Assessment for Clinically Significant Prostate Cancer: A STARD-Compliant Two-Center Study.
included_records.abstract: A total of 346 patients from center 1 and 292 patients from center 2. 3.0T scanners, T2-weighted imaging (T2WI), diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), and dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) imaging. Four radiologists interpreted the Bp-MRI (T2WI and DWI) and Mp-MRI (T2WI, DWI, and DCE) independently according to the Prostate Imaging Reporting and Data System (PI-RADS). Pathologies at biopsy and/or prostatectomy specimens were used as standard references. Mp-MRI sensitivity (senior/junior): 92.3% [265/287] / 93.7% [269/287]; specificity: 67.8% [238/351] / 68.1% [239/351].

included_records.title: Artificial intelligence and radiologists in prostate cancer detection on MRI (PI-CAI): an international, paired, non-inferiority, confirmatory study.
included_records.abstract: We trained and externally validated an AI system for detecting Gleason grade group 2 or greater cancers using a retrospective cohort of 10,207 MRI examinations from 9129 patients. 1000 cases from four centres (Netherlands and Norway) were used for testing. In all 1000 testing cases where the AI system was compared with the radiology readings made during multidisciplinary practice, non-inferiority was not confirmed; the AI system showed lower specificity (68.9% [95% CI 65.3-72.4] vs 69.0% [65.5-72.5]) at the same sensitivity (96.1%, 94.0-98.2) as the PI-RADS 3 or greater operating point. Of 10,207 examinations, 2440 (23.9%) had histologically confirmed Gleason grade group 2 or greater prostate cancer.

included_records.title: Detection of clinically significant prostate cancer with 18F-DCFPyL PET/multiparametric MR.
included_records.journal: Eur J Nucl Med Mol Imaging
included_records.abstract: 55 men with suspicion of PCa and negative systematic biopsies or clinically discordant low-risk PCa (n=21) or those being considered for FT (n=34) received 18F-DCFPyL PET/mpMR. There were 45/55 patients (81.8%) with histologically proven PCa and 41/55 (74.5%) diagnosed with csPCa. Overall, 61/114 lesions (53.5%) identified on any modality were malignant; 49/61 lesions (80.3%) were csPCa. On lesion-level analysis, for detection of csPCa, the sensitivity of PET was higher than mpMR and PET/MR (86% vs 67% and 69%), but at a lower specificity (32% vs 85% and 86%).

excluded_records.reason: PRECISION trial reports detection-rate (paired RCT) not classic 2x2 vs reference standard; abstract gives proportion-detected but not Sens/Spec against composite histology reference for the MRI arm only
Maxeiner 2018 - only PI-RADS>=3 men underwent biopsy, so no MRI- denominator for Spec calculation
VISIONING - opportunistic screening; reference standard discordant for negative bpMRI (template biopsy only on suspicious DRE/PSA)

Oerther 2024 - systematic review/meta-analysis pooling primary studies; cited in Discussion as the canonical Cochrane-style benchmark; primaries already individually included in our pool where extractable
Metser 2021 - lesion-level (not patient-level), small N; placed in sensitivity tier with caveat
Mahajan 2022 - very small N=36; bp-MRI vs mp-MRI within-patient comparison; specificity not reported with denominator

search_strategy_notes: Negation-context guard applied: each Sens%/Spec% match scanned +/-30 chars for negation tokens (not, non, never, excluding); none triggered.

csPCa definition heterogeneity flagged in extraction audit: PROMIS uses Gleason >=4+3 / ISUP >=3 (more stringent), all other studies use ISUP >=2 / Gleason >=3+4. This is a structural heterogeneity axis - retained as a data caveat on PROMIS. Subgroup tab exposes pirads_threshold and reference_standard_specific axes.
